name: Umasvati
dates_notes: Dated by his auto-commentary's mention as a settled work in early-fifth-century Jaina sources and by his absorption of pre-existing Āgama-material; Bronkhorst, Dundas (1992, The Jains), and Wiley converge on the second to fourth century CE. Both Digambara and Śvetāmbara sectarian traditions claim him; the modern scholarly view treats his date as bridging the sectarian split (formalised c. 79 CE) but with the surviving textual recensions reflecting later sub-traditions.

lineage_polemical.note: Brahma-Sūtra 2.2.33–36 refutes the Jaina position broadly conceived: the anekānta thesis is rejected on the ground that contradictory predicates cannot belong to a single substance, and the doctrine of the soul's pradeśa-extension to body-size is rejected as incoherent. Umāsvāti is the foundational figure of the position refuted.

Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 2.2.33–36 develops Bādarāyaṇa's anti-Jaina argument: Śaṅkara argues that anekānta-vāda either collapses into self-contradiction or trivializes into the unreal thesis that everything is somehow both A and not-A from different standpoints.

core_thesis: Umāsvāti's *Tattvārtha-Sūtra* — the only Sanskrit-language foundational treatise of Jainism, composed in the *sūtra*-style of the other *darśanas* — is accepted as authoritative by both Digambara and Śvetāmbara sub-traditions, with minor textual variants. The work's first *sūtra* — *samyag-darśana-jñāna-cāritrāṇi mokṣa-mārgaḥ*, "right vision, right knowledge, right conduct are the path to liberation" — fixes the Jaina *triratna*. The metaphysics is articulated through the seven *tattvas*: *jīva* (soul), *ajīva* (non-soul), *āsrava* (karmic influx), *bandha* (bondage), *saṃvara* (stoppage), *nirjarā* (shedding), *mokṣa* (liberation). *Jīva* is plural, ontologically distinct, possessed of *upayoga* (consciousness in its cognitive-conative aspect) and *cetanā*; in the embodied state it is *pradeśa*-coextensive with the body. *Ajīva* decomposes into five categories: *pudgala* (matter, the only category with mass), *dharma* and *adharma* (the media of motion and rest), *ākāśa*, and *kāla*. The epistemology recognises five kinds of *jñāna* (*mati*, *śruta*, *avadhi*, *manaḥ-paryāya*, *kevala*) and is informed by *anekānta-vāda* (the doctrine of manifold standpoints) and *syād-vāda* (the doctrine that any predication must be qualified by *syāt*, "in some respect"). *Sarva-jña*-doctrine — that the *kevalin*'s knowledge is unrestricted in object — is articulated as the apex of cognitive perfection. The text is Jainism's ontology, epistemology, ethics, and soteriology compressed into a single shared sūtra-grammar.

engaged_works.title: Tattvartha-Sutra
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composition fixed by Pūjyapāda's Sarvārtha-Siddhi (sixth century, Digambara) and Siddhasenagaṇi's Bhāṣya (sixth century, Śvetāmbara) treating the text as a settled authority.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely ascribed to Umāsvāti by uniform Digambara and Śvetāmbara tradition; the Śvetāmbara recension also accepts an auto-commentary (the Tattvārtha-Bhāṣya), the Digambara recension treats only the sūtras as Umāsvāti's work and reads them through Pūjyapāda's commentary.

ascription_evidence.description: Dundas (1992, The Jains); Wiley; the cross-sectarian acceptance of the sūtras (with minor variants) is itself the strongest evidence.

engaged_works.summary: Ten *adhyāyas* of compressed *sūtras* covering the entire Jaina *darśana*. *Adhyāya* 1 establishes the *triratna* (right vision, knowledge, conduct), the seven *tattvas*, the five kinds of *jñāna*, and the *naya* — *anekānta*-*syād-vāda* framework. *Adhyāya* 2 analyses *jīva* — its *upayoga*, the four *gatis* (states of existence), the *prāṇas*, *pradeśa*-extension. *Adhyāya* 3 treats the *adho-loka* (lower world). *Adhyāya* 4 treats the *deva-loka*. *Adhyāya* 5 treats *ajīva* — *pudgala*, *dharma*, *adharma*, *ākāśa*, *kāla* — and the analysis of *paramāṇus* and their aggregation. *Adhyāya* 6 treats *āsrava* (karmic influx) and the eight *karma-prakṛtis*. *Adhyāya* 7 treats the lay-vows (*aṇuvratas*) and the householder's path. *Adhyāya* 8 treats *bandha* (karmic bondage) and the duration, intensity, and quantity of bound *karma*. *Adhyāya* 9 treats *saṃvara* (stoppage) and *nirjarā* (shedding) — the disciplines of *gupti*, *samiti*, *parīṣaha-jaya*, *cāritra*, and the twelve *tapas*. *Adhyāya* 10 treats *mokṣa*: the *kevalin*'s state, the upward motion of the liberated *jīva* to the *siddha-loka*. The Digambara recension's *Sarvārtha-Siddhi* (Pūjyapāda) and the Śvetāmbara recension's *Tattvārtha-Bhāṣya* (Umāsvāti's auto-commentary) and *Bhāṣya-Ṭīkā* (Siddhasenagaṇi) are the principal pre-modern commentaries.
